posts.content:
New report is out with the latest open model adoption data we have gathered for Interconnects & The ATOM Project. At the surface level, we can see Chinese models continuing to accelerate in adoption. The report details much more. 
atomproject.ai/report

1. dont fall for anti open model fearmongering, but
2. acknowledge that AI capabilities are proceeding fast, and eventually there may be a reason to be more careful with open weight models

I don't think Mythos is that trigger, but I'm not 100% confident
www.interconnects.ai/p/claude-myt...

My book, Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback, is wrapping up and going into final production (copyediting, making pretty, formatting, etc.). Shipping to you in 1-2 months!

It's a wonderful project to create a foundation of knowledge for the research communities that I love and operate in.

Directionally, I agree with this piece, but it's important to note that this is the first blip in a long, slow transition towards a more hybrid model of open and closed models. 
www.chinatalk.media/p/chinas-ai-...
Incredible video. Very pure experience in why so many of us love science. www.youtube.com/watch?v=oaXR...

Great stuff happening as we start to build out the codebases for my RLHF book (sorry, I haven't had much time until now!). Very accessible to issues, emails, comments etc to make it better. I'm also going to need another dgx spark. 
github.com/natolambert/...

In 5-10 years, as models get more expensive & capable, I see the funding structures and support for open models breaking down. We need to consider if we need other options of supporting the open ecosystem.

The inevitable need for an open model consortium
www.interconnects.ai/p/the-inevit...
a bit over 7 days out from the Gemma 4 release and it's models are outpacing (slightly) the equivalent Qwen 3.5 models on downloads. Big numbers!

blogs.title: Claude Mythos and misguided open-weight fearmongering
blogs.author: Nathan Lambert
blogs.summary: Another dance around fears of open-source.
blogs.highlights: The post critiques the 'Claude Mythos' narrative that overstates risks of open-weight AI models, arguing it's a form of fearmongering that distracts from more substantive discussions. It suggests this pattern reflects recurring anxieties in open-source debates rather than new, evidence-based concerns.
blogs.worthReading: It offers a critical perspective on current AI discourse, challenging common assumptions about open-source risks and encouraging more nuanced evaluation of model accessibility.

blogs.title: The inevitable need for an open model consortium
blogs.author: Nathan Lambert
blogs.summary: And yes, I hate consortia too.
blogs.highlights: The article argues that despite general skepticism toward consortia, the AI field urgently requires an open model consortium to ensure transparency, collaboration, and ethical standards. This collective approach is framed as essential for addressing the rapid, often opaque advancements in AI development.
blogs.worthReading: It offers a pragmatic perspective on overcoming industry fragmentation and highlights the critical role of open collaboration in shaping responsible AI innovation.

interestSummary: Nathan Lambert is focused on the evolving landscape of open and closed AI models, analyzing adoption trends and advocating for the open ecosystem's sustainability. He is also finalizing his book on Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback and developing its accompanying codebases.
